papers.title: Very Wide Binary Stars as the Primary Source of Stellar Collisions in the Galaxy
papers.author_names: Nathan A. Kaib Sean N. Raymond

abstract.text: We present numerical simulations modeling the orbital evolution of very wide binaries, pairs of stars separated by over ~1000 AU. Due to perturbations from other passing stars and the Milky Way's tide, the orbits of very wide binary stars occasionally become extremely eccentric, which forces close encounters between the companion stars (Kaib et al. 2013). We show that this process causes a stellar collision between very wide binary companion stars once every 1000-7500 years on average in the Milky Way. One of the main uncertainties in this collision rate is the amount of energy dissipated by dynamic tides during close (but not collisional) periastron passages. This dissipation presents a dynamical barrier to stellar collisions and can instead transform very wide binaries into close or contact binaries. However, for any plausible tidal dissipation model, very wide binary stars are an unrealized, and potentially the dominant, source of stellar collisions in our Galaxy. Such collisions should occur throughout the thin disk of the Milky Way. Stellar collisions within very wide binaries should yield a small population of single, Li-depleted, rapidly rotating massive stars.

source_fields.sections_text: 6. Summary: External perturbations can drive very wide binary stars into stellar collisions, with a predicted collision rate of once every 1000–7500 years in the Milky Way, despite tidal dissipation limiting the rate and transforming many binaries into tight or contact systems, while also potentially producing rare, rapidly rotating, Li-rich stars. 1. Introduction: This paper introduces two novel collision scenarios—evolving triple star systems with AGB mass loss and Kozai-Lidov oscillations with enhanced periastron shifts—demonstrating that stellar collisions can frequently occur in Milky Way field environments, significantly expanding the known habitats for such events beyond globular clusters and the Galactic center. 2. Numerical Methods: The paper proposes a large-scale numerical simulation method that models the long-term orbital evolution of 500,000 very wide binary star systems using a symplectic integrator, with each batch of 1,000 binaries subjected to tidal perturbations from the Milky Way's disk and repeated impulse approximations from field star passages, where the same set of stellar encounters is reused across batches to improve computational efficiency. 3. Simulation Results: Simulations show that stellar collisions in very wide binaries occur in a tiny fraction of orbital configurations, with a median collision probability of 1 in ~500 among Galactic binaries, leading to one collision every ~10³ years in the Milky Way due to perturbations from the Galactic tide and passing stars, and the collision rate varies by nearly an order of magnitude with local galactic density. 4. An Analytical Understanding: This section derives the collision probability of very wide binaries as a function of semimajor axis, showing that while galactic tides are ineffective due to negligible periastron changes at small values, collisions are primarily driven by impulsive perturbations from passing field stars, with the loss cone fraction governed by tidal circularization and a dynamic tidal barrier. 5. Potential Observational Signature: Stellar collisions between low-mass stars can produce rare, rapidly rotating, lithium-depleted high-mass stars that provide a potential observational signature and explanation for anomalous stars not accounted for by conventional star formation.

Due to perturbations from other passing stars and the Milky Way's tide, the orbits of very wide binary stars occasionally become extremely eccentric, which forces close encounters between the companion
We show that this process causes a stellar collision between very wide binary companion stars once every 1000-7500 years on average in the Milky Way.
Stellar collisions within very wide binaries should yield a small population of single, Li-depleted, rapidly rotating massive stars.
title: Very Wide Binary Stars as the Primary Source of Stellar Collisions in the Galaxy

catalog_assessment.evidence: Purely theoretical numerical simulation paper modeling orbital evolution of very wide binaries and collision rates. No observational data or object-level stellar catalog presented.

papers.title: N-body simulations of globular clusters in tidal fields: Effects of intermediate-mass black holes
papers.author_names: N. Lützgendorf H. Baumgardt J. M. D. Kruijssen

abstract.text: Intermediate-mass black holes (IMBHs) may provide the missing link to understanding the growth of supermassive black holes in the early Universe. Some formation scenarios predict that IMBHs could have formed by runaway collisions in globular clusters (GCs). Understanding the influence of an IMBH in the center of a GC on its environment might provide indirect detection methods. Our goal is to test the effects of different initial compositions of GCs on their evolution in a tidal field. We pin down the crucial observables that indicate the presence of an IMBH at the center of the cluster. In addition to central IMBHs, we also consider the effects of different stellar-mass black hole retention and stellar binary fractions. We performed a set of 22 N-body simulations and varied particle numbers, IMBH masses, stellar-mass black-hole retention fractions, and stellar binary fractions. These models are all run in an external tidal field to study the effect of black holes on the cluster mass loss, mass function, and life times. Finally, we compared our results with observational data. We found that a central massive black hole increases the escape rate of high-mass stars from a star cluster, implying that the relative depletion of the mass function at the low-mass end proceeds less rapidly. Furthermore, we found a similar behavior for a cluster hosting a high number of stellar-mass black holes instead of one massive central IMBH. The presence of an IMBH also weakly affects the fraction of the cluster mass that is constituted by stellar remnants, as does the presence of primordial binaries. We compared our simulations with observational data from the literature and found good agreement between our models and observed mass functions and structural parameters of GCs. We exploited this agreement to identify GCs that could potentially host IMBHs.

source_fields.sections_text: Models: The paper proposes a suite of high-resolution $N$-body simulations using GPU-accelerated code with KS and chain regularization to study the dynamical evolution of star clusters, focusing on the effects of intermediate-mass black hole masses, binary fractions, and black hole retention on cluster evolution and core collapse. Observables: This section analyzes observables—specifically cluster lifetimes and remnant fractions—affected by intermediate-mass black holes, showing that IMBHs have only a modest impact on these properties compared to other factors like binary fractions and retention rates. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel framework that quantifies how the presence of an intermediate-mass black hole alters the stellar mass function and mass-to-light ratio of evaporating globular clusters, offering a new observational tracer for IMBHs beyond kinematic or surface brightness analyses. Summary and discussion: Clusters with intermediate-mass black holes or high primordial binary fractions exhibit longer lifetimes, delayed depletion of low-mass stars, and lower mass function slopes with reduced concentrations, with IMBH models best explaining observed GCs having low concentrations and depleted mass functions, particularly those in the upper left of the α–c plane. Comparison with observed GCs: This section compares simulated globular clusters' mass function slopes ($\\alpha$) and concentration ($c$) with observational data, finding a contradiction between theoretical expectations of a positive $\\alpha$–$c$ correlation and observed anti-correlation, which may indicate the need for additional physical processes or new cluster candidates hosting intermediate-mass black holes.
